data.title: SkyWest (SKYW) Margins Hold Above 10% In Q1 2026 Challenging Cautious Narratives
data.description:
SkyWest (SKYW) has kicked off Q1 2026 with total revenue of US$1.0b, basic EPS of US$2.54 and net income of US$101.7 million, setting the tone for its latest set of results. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$948.5 million in Q1 2025 to US$1.0b in Q1 2026, while basic EPS has shifted from US$2.48 to US$2.54 over the same period, against a backdrop of trailing 12 month EPS of US$10.69 and net income of US$429.5 million. With a trailing net margin of 10.4% versus 9.9% the prior year, investors may view this update as a reflection of earnings momentum and firmer profitability.

See our full analysis for SkyWest.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings picture lines up against the prevailing narratives around SkyWest's growth potential, risk profile and long term profit trajectory.

See what the community is saying about SkyWest

Advertisement

Margins Hold Above 10% Despite Softer Load Factor

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, SkyWest generated US$4.1b of revenue and US$429.5 million of net income, which works out to a 10.4% net margin compared with 9.9% the prior year.
  • Consensus narrative argues that stronger demand in regional markets and fleet modernization should support margins, and the current 10.4% margin alongside a Q1 2026 passenger load factor of 78% tests that view:
    • The margin improvement from 9.9% to 10.4% lines up with the idea of better efficiency, even though the Q1 2026 load factor is lower than the 81.5% trailing figure reported at Q4 2025.
    • Analysts also expect margins to move from about 10.6% to 11.5% over the next few years, so the current level gives some support to that expectation but does not yet reflect the higher margin band they are talking about.

Valuation Gap Versus DCF And Targets

  • SkyWest trades at US$86.09 with a P/E of 8.1x, against an industry average of 8.2x and a peer average of 29.2x. A DCF fair value of US$341.60 and an analyst consensus price target of US$123.83 both sit well above the current share price.
  • Supporters of the bullish view point to the combination of these valuation markers and trailing earnings growth of 18.2% as backing their case, and the current numbers give them several talking points:
    • The large gap between the US$86.09 price and the US$341.60 DCF fair value is consistent with the idea that the stock could be pricing in much less than the modelled future cash flows.
    • The P/E of 8.1x being well below the cited peer average of 29.2x is the kind of relative valuation bulls highlight when they argue the market is not fully reflecting the recent 18.2% earnings growth.
Have a closer look at how optimistic investors justify that gap between current price, DCF fair value and analyst targets by reading the 🐂 SkyWest Bull Case.

Debt Load And Insider Selling Keep Bears Cautious

  • Alongside the stronger trailing profit picture, the risk summary flags a high level of debt and significant insider selling over the last three months, while analysts still expect earnings to grow about 6.9% per year and revenue about 3.7% per year.
  • Bears focus on those balance sheet and insider trends, and the numbers here give them some concrete anchors even as margins improve:
    • The combination of a 10.4% net margin and 18.2% earnings growth over the past year sits against the reminder that leverage remains high, so part of the story is about how much of that profit has to support existing obligations.
    • Recent insider selling next to a share price of US$86.09 and an analyst target of US$123.83 lets skeptics argue that not all insiders are acting as if that upside will automatically play out, even with the current earnings momentum.
If you want to see how cautious investors build their case around leverage, insider moves and more moderate growth assumptions, check out the 🐻 SkyWest Bear Case.

Next Steps

To see how these results tie into long-term growth, risks, and valuation, check out the full range of community narratives for SkyWest on Simply Wall St. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio so you'll be alerted when the story evolves.

Seen enough to sense both optimism and caution in the story so far? Take a moment to weigh the trade off yourself with 5 key rewards and 2 important warning signs.

See What Else Is Out There

SkyWest's high debt levels and recent insider selling mean some investors may question how durable the current profit picture and valuation case really are.

data.title: Is International Flavors & Fragrances (IFF) Offering Value After Years Of Share Price Weakness
data.description:
• For readers wondering whether International Flavors & Fragrances is attractively priced right now or if the recent share moves are already baking in the story, this article walks through the numbers so you can judge the value for yourself.
  • The stock closed at US$71.14, with a recent 7-day return of a 6.2% decline, a 30-day return of 0.6%, a year-to-date return of 4.6%, and a 1-year return of a 4.4% decline that sits against a 3-year return of a 20.6% decline and a 5-year return of a 42.8% decline.

This context helps explain why the share price has seen both shorter-term resilience and longer-term pressure.

Approach 1: International Flavors & Fragrances Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, giving an estimate of what the business might be worth right now.

For International Flavors & Fragrances, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about $345.5 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts out to 2028, where free cash flow is projected at $1.15 billion. Simply Wall St then extrapolates this path further out to 2035 using gradually moderating growth assumptions.

After discounting these cash flows back to today, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $119.22 per share, compared with the recent share price of $71.14. That gap implies the stock is trading at roughly a 40.3% discount to this DCF estimate. This indicates potential upside if the cash flow path and discount rate prove reasonable.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests International Flavors & Fragrances is undervalued by 40.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 56 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for International Flavors & Fragrances.

Approach 2: International Flavors & Fragrances Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings can be affected by non cash items or one off charges, a Price to Sales, or P/S, multiple is often useful because it focuses on the revenue that the business generates rather than the bottom line. Investors usually pay a higher or lower P/S depending on what they expect for future growth and how risky they believe those sales are, so a "normal" level tends to reflect both prospects and perceived stability.

International Flavors & Fragrances currently trades on a P/S of 1.67x. That sits below the average for its Chemicals industry, which stands at 1.11x, and below the average of its peer group at 2.34x. Simply Wall St also provides a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 1.88x for the company, which is the P/S multiple suggested by factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can give a more tailored anchor than a simple industry or peer comparison because it adjusts for the company’s own profile instead of assuming it should trade like the group. Comparing the current 1.67x P/S to the 1.88x Fair Ratio indicates the shares are trading below that modelled level.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives bring your view of International Flavors & Fragrances together with the numbers by letting you write a clear story about the business, link that story to explicit forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins, and translate those forecasts into a Fair Value you can compare with the current share price.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives are an accessible tool used by many investors to set out their assumptions, see a Fair Value based on those inputs, and then quickly judge whether the shares look expensive or inexpensive relative to that Fair Value. This can help you decide whether it might be the right time to buy more, trim, or simply watch.

Narratives also respond when new information arrives. If International Flavors & Fragrances reports earnings, updates 2026 guidance, or announces portfolio moves, the Fair Value linked to that Narrative refreshes automatically and keeps your story and numbers aligned without extra effort.

For example, one Narrative on Simply Wall St currently assigns a Fair Value of US$100.00 to International Flavors & Fragrances while another assigns US$77.61. data.title: Oracle’s US$16b Michigan AI Campus Raises New Questions For Investors
data.description:
• Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) has secured US$16b in financing for a large AI data center campus in Michigan.
  • The project involves Related Digital and Blackstone as key development and financing partners.
  • The campus is designed to support Oracle's dedicated AI infrastructure for OpenAI and other customers in the US.

Oracle is best known for its database software, cloud infrastructure and enterprise applications, and this Michigan campus fits into its push to supply compute for AI workloads. Across the industry, large cloud providers are committing significant capital to AI-focused data centers as customers look for more capacity for training and running AI models. For investors tracking NYSE:ORCL, this financing move shows how the company is positioning its cloud platform within the broader build-out of AI infrastructure.

The new campus is intended to expand Oracle's footprint in US AI compute and support its relationships with OpenAI and other AI leaders. Investors may want to watch how quickly Oracle translates this build-out into contracted capacity, usage trends and longer-term cloud agreements, as those factors can influence how this infrastructure contributes to overall cloud and AI-related revenue over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Oracle by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Oracle.

Is Oracle's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

The US$16b financing package for the Michigan AI campus is a sizeable addition to Oracle’s broader plan to raise US$45b to US$50b in 2026 for data center expansion, and it highlights how much of that buildout is being supported by project-specific debt and partner equity rather than solely on Oracle’s own balance sheet. With Related Digital and Blackstone providing equity and PIMCO-managed funds anchoring long term, fixed rate debt, much of the funding and asset ownership sits at the project level. For you as an investor, the key questions are how Oracle’s contractual commitments and take-or-pay style arrangements are structured, how the related lease or capacity payments show up in future operating expenses, and how they interact with existing concerns that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow. This financing can support Oracle’s AI capacity goals for customers like OpenAI, but it also adds another large, long duration obligation into a capital program that already includes sizeable data center loans arranged by major banks.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Oracle Narrative

  • The Michigan financing supports the narrative that Oracle is building large scale, AI focused infrastructure to serve a sizeable backlog of contracts for AI workloads and multicloud database services.
  • It also underscores a key concern in the narrative that very heavy, debt backed infrastructure spending could pressure free cash flow if AI demand or customer usage patterns differ from expectations.
  • The specific role of third party equity partners and project level debt in structuring these campuses is not fully reflected in the narrative, which mainly focuses on total CapEx and remaining performance obligations.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Oracle to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged that Oracle’s debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, and large project financings for AI campuses add to future obligations that need strong, sustained utilization to be comfortable.
  • ⚠️ Heavy reliance on a small group of AI customers for very large, long term infrastructure commitments could become a problem if any of those partners scale back, delay or redirect workloads to rivals such as Microsoft Azure, Amazon Web Services or Google Cloud.
  • 🎁 Some investors see Oracle trading at roughly 35% below one estimate of fair value, with earnings expected to grow at around 24% a year, and large AI focused data center projects like Michigan are part of the capacity required to support that growth outlook.
  • 🎁 The use of long term, fixed rate, project specific debt with external partners can help Oracle secure the AI capacity its customers want while avoiding taking the entire US$16b commitment directly on its own balance sheet.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth tracking how quickly Oracle converts AI data center projects such as Michigan into active, revenue generating capacity, and how that shows up in cloud infrastructure and AI related revenue lines. Pay attention to any disclosures on lease or service payments tied to these partner financed campuses, updates on remaining performance obligations attached to AI workloads, and commentary on free cash flow given the concerns that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow. It is also useful to compare Oracle’s approach to AI capacity and debt funding with what peers like Microsoft, Amazon and Google are doing, particularly around balance sheet risk and project structures.

Approach 1: Broadcom Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a present value. It focuses on the cash that could theoretically be returned to shareholders over time.

For Broadcom, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $28.9b.
